ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai Pandan Chicken; mapped to the broader 'chicken dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pandan-wrapped deep-fried chicken; A0CKM (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Thai Pandan Chicken as a prepared dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Thai Pandan Chicken consists of 3 to 4 pandan-wrapped chicken parcels, weighing approximately 150 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Pandan Chicken — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai Pandan Chicken (cr_id G00010753). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for deep-fried marinated chicken (USDA #05062) as a proportional analogue; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai Pandan Chicken. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed chicken dish with moderate carbohydrate content (14.4 g/100g) from marinade ingredients including sugar and oyster sauce. The carbohydrate matrix is dominated by small amounts of added sugars and starch from sauces, which are partially offset by protein (8.1 g) and fat (7.5 g) that slow gastric emptying. Reference anchor: mixed meat dish with sauce, GI ~45, from University of Sydney GI Database analogous category. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (45 × 14.36 × 1.5) / 100 = 9.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 384 mg/100g with insufficient potassium (152 mg/100g) and fibre (1.2 g/100g) offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 384 mg/100g sits in the borderline-to-high range; potassium at 152 mg/100g and dietary fibre at 1.2 g/100g both fall below the thresholds needed to offset sodium risk, making this dish unsuitable for hypertensive individuals unless portion is strictly limited and preparation is modified to reduce fish sauce and oyster sauce quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (14.4 g/100g) and sugar (4.1 g/100g) with deep-frying increasing caloric density and fat load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Carbohydrate at 14.4 g/100g and sugar at 4.1 g/100g are within acceptable limits for type 2 diabetes management, but the deep-frying method increases total fat and caloric density, which can worsen insulin resistance over time; individuals with type 2 diabetes should request baked or air-fried preparation and limit portion to one serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (384 mg/100g), deep-frying fat load, and cholesterol (48 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of 384 mg sodium per 100g, deep-frying-derived fat (estimated saturated fat ~1.8 g/100g), and cholesterol at 48 mg/100g presents a compounded cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish in its standard deep-fried form and request baked or air-fried preparation with reduced sauce to lower sodium and fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Standard deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that directly exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; request baked or air-fried preparation to eliminate AGE formation, which would make the dish potentially suitable given the anti-inflammatory lemongrass and galangal content in the marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Pandan Chicken (ไก่ห่อใบเตย, Kai Ho Bai Toey) is a popular Thai and Singaporean dish consisting of bite-sized chicken pieces marinated in a fragrant blend of lemongrass, galangal, garlic, coriander root, fish sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, sugar, and white pepper, then individually wrapped in fresh pandan (screwpine) leaves and deep-fried until golden. The pandan leaf wrapping imparts a distinctive green colour, a subtle grassy-sweet aroma, and keeps the chicken moist during frying. The dish is commonly served as a starter or side dish at Thai restaurants and Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 154 kcal, 8.1 g protein, 7.5 g fat, 14.4 g carbohydrate, 4.1 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 384 mg sodium. The relatively high sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce makes it a concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. Deep-frying increases fat content and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), making it unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis unless prepared by baking or air-frying. For type 2 diabetes management, the moderate carbohydrate level is acceptable but the deep-frying method should be avoided. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts and is dairy-free. It is culturally associated with Thai cuisine but has been widely adopted in Singapore's multicultural food landscape.
common_user_queries: Is pandan chicken high in sodium?
What is Kai Ho Bai Toey?
How many calories in pandan chicken?
Is pandan chicken suitable for hypertension?
What are the ingredients in Thai pandan chicken?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010753, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from a USDA SR Legacy analogue and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category, clearly documented. All multilingual names are verified against standard culinary references.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for deep-fried marinated chicken (USDA #05062) as a proportional analogue, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This is documented in nutrient_source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this Thai Pandan Chicken preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Thai Pandan Chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 4. The input record lists primary_cuisine as 'Malay' but Thai Pandan Chicken is of Thai origin and widely adopted in Singapore; cuisine has been corrected to 'Thai / Singaporean' with appropriate multilingual coverage for both Thai (mandatory) and Singaporean (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) language requirements. 5. Halal status is marked true based on standard hawker-centre preparation in Singapore; individual vendors should be verified. 6. No core macro fields are zero; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.
